build.role_note: team supervisor + sole integrator + gate (senior engineer); delegates GLM 5.2 implementation to the general route

supervisor.note: Primary session: orchestrates, gates, integrates; may edit directly for small fixes; delegates implementation to the implementation tier.

implementation.note: The only tier granted edit/write/bash. All implementation work routes here; never to reasoning roles.

reasoning.note: Read-only. Sol and Fable (and Opus when used here) NEVER receive implementation briefs and cannot edit; they plan, review, and diagnose only.

fanout.note: Read-only small-model lanes for search, research, and compaction support.

implementation_pool.note: Up to 12 concurrent GLM 5.2 implementers split across makora and umans (both GLM 5.2); at least 6 on makora. Verified 2026-07-18: makora+umans GLM children dispatch concurrently with extensions:true.

leaf_excluded_skills.reason: interactive interrogation skills require operator dialogue; leaf workers cannot ask the operator, so these are never named in worker briefs and are primary-session-only

skill_policy.empty_means: no skill loaded; the worker proceeds with role defaults only. Kernel execution-discipline skills are considered first by the primary but are not auto-loaded — when relevant they must still be named in required_skills.

steps: primary reads the brief's required_skills (must be present; [] is valid)
primary resolves each entry against manifest.tiers (kernel | mandatory-on-trigger | specialist | high-context) and manifest.primary_only.interactive
unknown skill (absent from the catalog) -> reject the brief, name the unknown skill, do not spawn
primary attaches the resolved skills to the spawned brief

skill_policy.unknown_skill_action: reject: do not spawn; return the brief to the originator with the unknown skill named

leaf_restrictions.reason: leaf workers cannot ask the operator; interactive and primary-session-only skills are rejected from subagent briefs and rerouted to the primary session

skill_policy.role_defaults: none — no skill is hard-coded onto any role or loaded by default. Skills attach per-brief from required_skills only, matched by the primary against manifest intent_bundles and tier triggers. Kernel execution-discipline skills are always considered first but still require explicit naming in required_skills when relevant; no skill loads implicitly.
skill_policy.high_context_routing: high-context tier skills route to the primary or a large-model lane, never to a bounded leaf worker
skill_policy.authority: Skills never authorize commit, push, merge, publish, deploy, destructive, or other externally visible actions. AGENTS safety rules and operator confirmation dominate skill prose in every case; a skill's MUST/SHALL language scopes only the skill's own workflow, not operator-visible side effects.
skill_policy.enforcement_scope: Skill policy is enforced by the primary during dispatch resolution only — name validation, primary-only and leaf-exclusion checks before spawn. No skill gate is enforced at runtime inside the worker beyond the role's tool authority; the worker loads named skills into context and follows their prose.

quality_policy.scope: language-agnostic; applies to every implementation brief regardless of language
quality_policy.carried_by: the general.md contract injected into every implementation spawn — no extra brief field needed
rules: idiomatic to the target language (its formatter/linter defaults); match surrounding style

apply the language/framework's accepted best practices for correctness, security, error handling, resource cleanup, and clear naming, right-sized to the task (not gold-plated); cover testable new behavior with a test
simplest thing that works; no speculative abstractions, options, or layers not asked for
reuse stdlib and existing patterns before adding a dependency or wrapper
validate only at real boundaries; no defensive code for impossible cases
no slop tells: comments restating code, over-long docstrings, Manager/Factory/Wrapper scaffolding, swallowed try/catch, dead compatibility shims
complexity must not exceed the task

enforcement.writer: the general (implementation) tier follows the standard while editing
enforcement.gate: the primary (build) rejects any integrated diff that violates it and re-dispatches for a simpler pass
enforcement.review: the review role flags concrete over-engineering as a maintainability finding (not style)
enforcement.envelope: implementation worker checks_run must include an entry named quality-pack; absence = failed verification

layers.L1: quality-gate.ts annotates edit/write tool results (never blocks)
layers.L2: quality-gate.ts blocks git commit while staged files fail packs
layers.L3: implementation worker checks_run must include required_check_name; primary rejects otherwise
layers.L4: /team verify allowlist carries the canonical command; primary re-runs at ROOT after integrating a wave

token_budgets.policy: unlimited by operator decision; 0 disables token ceilings while wall-clock, cycle, retry, and operator checkpoints remain enforced

wave_policy.path_ownership: exclusive per task within a wave; overlapping paths force sequential waves
wave_policy.dependent_waves: Wave N+1 starts only after every Wave N task reaches verified

retry_on: instant failure with empty provider environment (see docs/fabric-tuning.md dispatch failure triage)
retry_policy.fallback_order: role alternate_model first, then dispatch.implementation_models order

coordination.protocol: primary mechanically gates research -> mesh collaboration -> plan -> implement -> verify -> review -> integrate -> goal; board is an audit projection and primary remains sole writer/integrator

actors.policy: Persistent read-only mailbox actors receive addressed per-run mesh events. /team reuses a fixed architect+risk roster, performs one bounded cross-review hop, and reads actor messages before planning. Actors never write the run board or integrate; the primary remains sole writer/integrator. No automatic actor deletion.

team_mode.enforcement: executable same-cycle evidence ledger inside /team; every transition validates host run status, structured envelope, and phase-specific evidence before dispatching the next role

implement.scale: one worker per genuinely independent non-overlapping unit

team_mode.evidence_source: host-reported result status plus successful tool_execution_end events from the host-reported child log; worker prose alone never unlocks a phase
team_mode.failure_policy: missing, malformed, stale-cycle, failed-run, failed-tool, wrong-role, or incomplete evidence blocks advancement and is mirrored to the board
team_mode.utilization_policy: use every mandatory role in team mode; treat 12 implementation workers as a capacity ceiling, never a utilization target
